name: Back Hyperextension
description: A controlled spinal extension exercise performed on a hyperextension bench. Primarily targets the lower back (erector spinae), glutes, and hamstrings.
instructions: Position your hips on the pad with feet secured. Lower your torso under control while keeping a neutral spine. Raise your upper body until aligned with your legs. Avoid overextending your lower back.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Keep spine neutral and avoid hyperextending at the top. Move slowly and avoid jerky motions. Use caution with L5-S1 history.

name: Single-Leg Glute Bridge
description: A unilateral glute activation exercise performed on the floor. It strengthens the glutes, stabilizers, hamstrings, and improves lumbopelvic control.
instructions: Lie on your back with one knee bent and the other leg extended. Press through your heel and lift your hips while keeping your core engaged. Avoid arching your lower back. Lower slowly with control.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Do not overextend hips. Keep ribs down and avoid pushing through lower back. Move slowly.

name: Hanging Leg Raises (Bent-Knee or Single-Leg)
description: A core exercise performed while hanging from a bar. The bent-knee or single-leg variation targets the abdominals and hip flexors while reducing lumbar strain.
instructions: Hang from a bar with shoulders active. Bend your knees and lift them toward your chest while keeping your core braced. Avoid swinging and keep your spine neutral. Lower slowly with control.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Use bent-knee or single-leg only. Avoid straight-leg raises. Keep pelvis neutral and avoid lumbar rounding.

name: One-Arm Crossover Cable Pull
description: A unilateral cable pulling exercise that targets the lats, upper back, and deep stabilizers. This movement also improves core control and supports a healthy spine.
instructions: Stand sideways to the cable machine with the handle set slightly above shoulder level. Grab the handle with the outside arm. Pull the cable across your body while keeping your torso stable and ribs down. Focus on driving the elbow toward the hip. Avoid rotating the spine.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Keep core braced and avoid twisting the lower back. Use light to moderate weight and move with control.

name: Cable Kickbacks
description: A controlled cable kickback exercise focusing on glutes, hamstrings, spinal stabilizers, and leg biceps. Great for strength without loading the lumbar spine.
instructions: Attach the ankle strap to the cable. Stand tall, brace your core, keep ribs down. Kick the leg backward slowly, avoid leaning forward or arching the back. Return with control.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Do not bend forward. Keep posture upright. Use light resistance and prioritize slow controlled movement.

name: Bird Dog (Bench or Stability Ball)
description: A cable-free core stabilization exercise that strengthens the spine, back muscles, and deep stabilizers with minimal lumbar load.
instructions: On a bench or stability ball, keep pelvis neutral. Extend opposite arm and leg slowly. Avoid shifting hips or arching lower back. Hold 2 seconds, switch sides.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Do not raise leg too high. Keep abdomen braced, ribs down, and move slow for stability.

name: Cable Knee Drive
description: The Cable Knee Drive is a dynamic lower-body exercise that strengthens the hip flexors, challenges the core, and enhances overall stability. With the cable strap secured around the ankle, you lift the knee toward the chest while resisting the pull of the machine. This movement closely resembles sprinting mechanics, making it especially valuable for improving acceleration, jump performance, and explosive leg power. The constant tension also forces the core to stay active, promoting better balance, coordination, and upright posture throughout the exercise.
instructions: Lie on your back, attach foot/handle to cable set at low pull. Brace core, drive one knee toward chest without lumbar rounding. Extend back slowly. Do not swing.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Perform single-leg only. Keep lower back on floor and pelvis neutral. Avoid lumbar rounding. Use light load.

name: Bodyweight/Light Dumbbell Bulgarian Split Squats
description: A back-friendly unilateral leg builder that avoids axial spinal loading while still building muscle effectively.
instructions: Place rear foot on bench, step forward, brace core. Lower under control without leaning torso forward aggressively.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Keep torso upright, avoid deep forward bend. No added weight if pain increases.

name: Leg Extension
description: Quads isolation using a machine with zero compression through the lumbar spine.
instructions: Sit tall, hold handles, extend knees under control. Avoid leaning back aggressively.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Do not slam the pad, keep movement smooth. Reduce range if nerve pain flares.

name: Seated Hip Abduction
description: Glute medius and outer-leg isolation improving hip stability and pelvis control, which supports spine health.
instructions: Sit tall, press knees outward slowly, avoid lumbar movement while spreading.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Keep pelvis neutral, avoid leaning sideways or arching.

name: Bench or Machine Hip Thrust
description: A glute and hamstring builder with external support that avoids spinal compression and trains hip hinge strength safely.
instructions: Upper back on bench or supported pad, drive hips up without lumbar arch. Ribs down, chin tucked slightly.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: No top-position lumbar arching. Reduce load if compression or nerve pain increases.

name: Belt Squats
description: A squat variation using a dip belt or dedicated belt-squat machine where weight hangs from the hips instead of resting on the shoulders. Removes spinal compression and is ideal for people with lower back concerns, herniated disc history, or shoulder injuries who want to load legs without compressing the spine.
instructions: Stand on the platform with the belt secured around your hips and weight attached. Keep your torso upright and core engaged. Lower into a squat by bending your knees and hips, keeping your chest up. Push through your feet to return to the starting position. The load stays on your pelvis/hips with minimal pressure on the lower back.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Keep torso upright and avoid excessive forward lean. Load is on pelvis, not spine. Great alternative for those with lumbar pain (L4-L5/L5-S1), herniated discs, or sciatica.
Excellent choice as it removes spinal compression. Weight hangs from hips, not shoulders. Maintain neutral spine throughout the movement.
Minimal pressure on lower back and discs. Keep movements controlled and avoid bouncing at the bottom. Stop if any nerve pain occurs.

name: Deadlift
description: A fundamental compound hip-hinge movement that targets the posterior chain including glutes, hamstrings, erector spinae, and upper back. While highly effective for building strength, proper form and load management are critical for spinal health.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, barbell over mid-foot. Hinge at hips, grip bar just outside legs. Brace core, keep spine neutral, chest up. Drive through heels, extend hips and knees simultaneously. Keep bar close to body. Lower with control by hinging hips first.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Use lighter loads (under 25kg starting weight) and perfect form. Keep spine neutral - no rounding or excessive arching. Stop immediately if pain occurs. Consider trap bar or Romanian deadlift variations.
Requires medical clearance. Use very light loads or avoid. Consider belt squats or hip thrusts instead.

Spinal flexion under load can aggravate sciatica. Avoid or use medical clearance only. Consider trap bar deadlifts, hip thrusts, or belt squats as safer alternatives that load the posterior chain without spinal compression.

name: Glute Bridge Machine
description: A machine-based or dumbbell-loaded glute bridge exercise that targets glutes and hamstrings with back support. Safer alternative to barbell hip thrusts for those with back concerns.
instructions: Position yourself on the machine or lie on your back with a dumbbell on your hips. Drive through heels to lift hips up, squeezing glutes at the top. Keep ribs down, avoid arching lower back. Lower with control.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Keep pelvis neutral at top position. Avoid overextending lumbar spine. Focus on glute contraction.

name: Seated Cable Row
description: A horizontal pulling exercise using a cable machine that targets the mid-back, lats, and rear delts. Excellent for building back thickness and improving posture while maintaining a stable, supported position.
instructions: Sit with feet on footrests, knees slightly bent. Grip handle with arms extended. Pull handle to lower abdomen, driving elbows back. Squeeze shoulder blades together. Keep torso upright, avoid rounding or overextending spine. Return with control.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Maintain upright posture throughout. Avoid flexion under load. Use smooth, controlled movements.

name: Chest Fly
description: An isolation exercise for the chest using a fly machine or lying with dumbbells. Targets pectorals with minimal shoulder stress when performed correctly.
instructions: Sit with back against pad, grip handles at chest level. Keep slight bend in elbows. Bring handles together in front of chest using a hugging motion. Control the return. Avoid excessive arching of lower back.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Keep lower back pressed against pad. Avoid driving through lower back to move weight.

name: Incline Leg Raises
description: A core exercise performed on an incline bench targeting the lower abdominals and hip flexors. The incline adds difficulty compared to flat leg raises.
instructions: Lie on incline bench, grip handles behind head. Keep lower back pressed to bench. Raise legs up toward chest with controlled movement. Lower slowly without letting lower back arch. Keep knees slightly bent if needed.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Keep lower back pressed to bench. Avoid excessive range that causes lumbar extension. Bend knees if needed.

name: Seated Dumbbell Shoulder Press
description: An overhead pressing exercise performed seated with dumbbells. Targets shoulders (deltoids) and triceps while the seated position provides back support.
instructions: Sit on bench with back support, hold dumbbells at shoulder height with palms forward. Press dumbbells overhead until arms are extended but not locked. Lower with control to shoulder level. Keep core engaged, avoid excessive arching of lower back.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Use back support. Avoid excessive arching. Start with lighter weights (3-4kg per dumbbell).

name: Dumbbell Lateral Raises
description: An isolation exercise for the side deltoids (shoulders) that creates shoulder width and definition. Minimal back involvement when performed correctly.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, dumbbells at sides. Keep slight bend in elbows. Raise arms out to sides until parallel with floor, leading with elbows. Lower with control. Avoid swinging or using momentum. Keep torso stable.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Use light weights (2-3kg). Avoid swinging or leaning. Keep torso stable.

name: Dumbbell Bicep Curls
description: A fundamental arm exercise targeting the biceps. Can be performed standing or seated with various grips (supinated, hammer, or alternating).
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart or sit on bench. Hold dumbbells with arms extended, palms forward. Curl weights up by bending elbows, keeping upper arms stationary. Squeeze biceps at top. Lower with control. Avoid swinging or using back momentum.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Avoid using momentum or swinging body. Keep core braced and spine neutral.

name: Dumbbell Tricep Kickbacks
description: An isolation exercise for the triceps performed in a hinged position. Targets the back of the arms with minimal shoulder involvement.
instructions: Hinge forward at hips with neutral spine, one hand on bench for support. Hold dumbbell with elbow bent at 90 degrees, upper arm parallel to torso. Extend arm back until fully straightened. Squeeze tricep. Return with control. Avoid swinging or moving upper arm.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Support torso on bench. Keep spine neutral, avoid rounding. Use light weights (2-3kg).

name: Cable Tricep Pushdown
description: A cable exercise targeting the triceps using a high pulley and straight bar, V-bar, or rope attachment. Excellent for building arm strength and definition.
instructions: Stand facing cable machine with high pulley. Grip bar with hands shoulder-width apart, elbows at sides. Push bar down by extending elbows until arms are straight. Keep elbows stationary. Return with control, stopping at 90 degrees. Maintain upright posture.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Stand upright, avoid leaning over bar. Keep core engaged and spine neutral.

name: French Bench Press (EZ-Bar Skullcrusher)
description: A lying triceps extension using an EZ-bar on a flat bench. Emphasizes long-head triceps with stable torso support, reducing lower-back demand.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench, feet planted. Grip an EZ-bar with a narrow, comfortable angle. Start with arms extended above shoulders. Bend elbows to lower the bar toward the forehead, keeping upper arms mostly vertical. Stop just before the bar reaches your forehead, then extend elbows to return. Keep elbows tucked and wrists neutral. Avoid arching the lower back.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Keep hips and ribs down on the bench. Use moderate load and controlled tempo. Stop if any low-back discomfort occurs.

name: Floor Crunches
description: A basic core exercise targeting the abdominals while lying on the floor or mat. Safer for the spine than sit-ups due to limited range of motion.
instructions: Lie on back with knees bent, feet flat on floor. Place hands behind head or across chest. Engage core and lift shoulder blades off floor by curling torso toward knees. Keep lower back on floor. Lower with control. Avoid pulling on neck.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Keep lower back on floor. Use controlled movements. Avoid jerky motions.

name: Bicycle Crunches
description: A dynamic core exercise that targets the abdominals and obliques through a rotating movement pattern. Excellent for overall core strength and coordination.
instructions: Lie on back, hands behind head, legs lifted with knees bent. Bring right elbow toward left knee while extending right leg. Alternate sides in a pedaling motion. Keep lower back pressed to floor. Move with control, not speed. Focus on rotation from torso, not just arms.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Keep lower back pressed to floor throughout. Slow controlled movements. Stop if any sharp pain.

name: Leg Press
description: A machine-based compound leg exercise that targets quads, glutes, and hamstrings with no spinal compression. Excellent alternative to squats for those with back issues.
instructions: Sit in leg press machine with back and head against pad. Place feet shoulder-width apart on platform. Push platform away by extending knees and hips. Lower with control, keeping knees aligned with toes. Avoid locking knees at top or rounding lower back at bottom.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Keep lower back pressed against pad throughout. Avoid excessive depth that causes tailbone to lift. Use moderate weight.
Excellent choice - no spinal loading. Stop if any nerve symptoms occur. Keep smooth controlled movements.

name: Lying Leg Curl
description: An isolation exercise for the hamstrings performed on a leg curl machine while lying face down. Targets hamstrings with zero spinal loading.
instructions: Lie face down on leg curl machine with pad behind ankles. Keep hips pressed to pad. Curl legs up toward glutes by bending knees. Squeeze hamstrings at top. Lower with control. Avoid lifting hips or arching back.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Keep hips and pelvis pressed to pad. No spinal movement or loading. Safe for most back conditions.

name: Single-Leg Romanian Deadlift
description: A unilateral hip hinge movement with light dumbbells that targets hamstrings, glutes, and balance. Reduces spinal load compared to barbell variations.
instructions: Stand on one leg holding light dumbbell in opposite hand. Hinge at hip, lowering dumbbell toward floor while extending free leg behind for balance. Keep back neutral, core braced. Return to standing by driving through heel. Use light weight and focus on form.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Use very light weight (5-8kg max). Keep spine neutral throughout. Stop if any pain occurs. Excellent for hamstring strengthening with minimal load.

name: Goblet Squat
description: A front-loaded squat holding a dumbbell or kettlebell at chest level. The front load promotes upright torso positioning and reduces lower back stress compared to back squats.
instructions: Hold dumbbell vertically at chest level with both hands. Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Squat down by bending knees and hips, keeping chest up and elbows inside knees. Lower to comfortable depth. Drive through heels to stand. Keep torso upright throughout.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Start with light weight (8-12kg). Front load keeps torso upright reducing lower back stress. Stop at comfortable depth - avoid excessive flexion.

name: Machine Chest Press
description: A machine-based horizontal pushing exercise targeting the chest, front delts, and triceps. Provides back support and stable movement path, safer than free weight bench press for those with back issues.
instructions: Sit in machine with back pressed firmly against pad. Grip handles at chest level. Push handles forward by extending arms until nearly straight. Return with control. Keep lower back pressed to pad throughout. Avoid arching back or driving through lower back.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Excellent choice with back support. Keep lower back pressed to pad. Avoid excessive arching. Safe for most back conditions.

name: Push-ups
description: A fundamental bodyweight exercise targeting chest, shoulders, and triceps while engaging the core. Can be modified with incline or knee variations for different difficulty levels.
instructions: Start in plank position with hands slightly wider than shoulders. Keep body in straight line from head to heels, core engaged. Lower chest toward floor by bending elbows. Push back up to starting position. Maintain neutral spine throughout - avoid sagging hips or piking up.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Requires good core stability. Keep neutral spine - avoid sagging or excessive arching. Start with incline push-ups if needed. Stop if any lower back pain occurs.

name: Assisted Dips
description: A compound upper body exercise targeting chest, triceps, and front delts using an assisted dip machine or resistance bands. The assistance reduces load on the lower back.
instructions: Position yourself on assisted dip machine with hands on handles, arms straight. Lean slightly forward for chest emphasis. Lower body by bending elbows until upper arms are parallel to floor. Push back up to starting position. Keep core engaged and avoid excessive swinging.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Use adequate assistance to reduce load. Keep core braced and avoid excessive swinging. Stop if lower back discomfort occurs.

name: Incline Dumbbell Press
description: An upper chest pressing exercise performed on an incline bench with dumbbells. The incline angle reduces lower back stress compared to flat pressing.
instructions: Set bench to 30-45 degree incline. Sit with back against pad, dumbbells at shoulder level. Press dumbbells up and slightly inward until arms are extended. Lower with control to shoulder level. Keep lower back pressed to pad, avoid excessive arching.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Incline reduces lower back stress. Use moderate weight. Keep back pressed to bench throughout. Avoid excessive arching.

name: Lat Pulldown (Neutral Grip)
description: A vertical pulling exercise using a lat pulldown machine with a neutral (palms facing) grip attachment. Targets the lats, upper back, and biceps while maintaining a stable seated position. Excellent for building back width and shoulder health with zero spinal compression.
instructions: Sit at lat pulldown machine with thighs secured under pad. Grip neutral handles with palms facing each other. Pull handles down toward upper chest by driving elbows down and back. Squeeze shoulder blades together at bottom. Return with control. Keep torso upright, avoid leaning back excessively.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Excellent choice - no spinal loading. Keep torso upright and core braced. Avoid excessive leaning back.
Safe exercise with seated support. Stop if any nerve symptoms occur.

name: Lat Pulldown (Wide Grip)
description: A vertical pulling exercise using a lat pulldown machine with a wide overhand grip. Emphasizes lat width and upper back development. The wide grip increases lat activation while the seated position provides stability.
instructions: Sit at lat pulldown machine with thighs secured. Grip wide bar with hands wider than shoulder-width, palms facing away. Pull bar down toward upper chest, focusing on driving elbows down. Squeeze lats at bottom. Return with control. Avoid pulling behind neck.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: No spinal compression. Keep core engaged and avoid excessive torso lean.

name: Pull-Up
description: A bodyweight vertical pulling exercise performed on a pull-up bar using an overhand grip. One of the most effective exercises for building back strength, lat development, and grip strength. Requires good upper body strength.
instructions: Hang from pull-up bar with overhand grip slightly wider than shoulders. Engage core and pull body up by driving elbows down until chin clears bar. Lower with control to full arm extension. Keep shoulders active at bottom, avoid dead hang. Use assistance or negative reps if needed.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: No spinal compression but requires core stability. Keep core braced to avoid swinging. Use assisted version if needed.

name: Chin-Up
description: A bodyweight vertical pulling exercise performed with an underhand (supinated) grip. Similar to pull-ups but with increased bicep activation. The underhand grip often makes this slightly easier than standard pull-ups.
instructions: Hang from bar with underhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart, palms facing you. Pull body up until chin clears bar, focusing on driving elbows down. Lower with control to full extension. Keep core engaged and avoid excessive swinging.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Requires core control. Keep body stable and avoid kipping. Use assisted version if needed.

name: Straight-Arm Pulldown
description: A lat isolation exercise using a cable machine with straight arms throughout the movement. Emphasizes lat engagement with minimal bicep involvement. Excellent for improving mind-muscle connection with the lats.
instructions: Stand facing high cable with rope or straight bar attachment. Grip handle with arms extended overhead, slight elbow bend. Keeping arms nearly straight, pull handle down toward thighs by contracting lats. Focus on driving elbows down. Return with control. Keep torso stable and core braced.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Keep upright posture and avoid leaning back excessively. Core should stay engaged throughout.

name: Assisted Pull-Up
description: A machine-assisted version of pull-ups that reduces the amount of bodyweight lifted. Allows beginners or those with back concerns to build pulling strength safely while working toward unassisted pull-ups.
instructions: Set assistance weight on machine. Kneel or stand on platform, grip handles with overhand grip. Pull body up until chin clears handles, focusing on driving elbows down. Lower with control. Gradually reduce assistance over time as strength improves.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Excellent option for building back strength with reduced load. Keep core engaged and movement controlled.

name: Cable Face Pull (Rope Face Pull)
description: A cable rear-delt and upper back exercise that improves shoulder health, posture, and external rotation strength. Essential for shoulder balance and injury prevention, especially for desk workers and those with forward shoulder posture.
instructions: Set cable at upper chest height. Attach rope. Grip ends with palms facing each other. Pull rope toward face, separating hands as they pass face level. Focus on squeezing shoulder blades together. Keep elbows high and pull rope to eye level. Return with control. Use light weight and high reps for best results.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Excellent choice - zero spinal loading. Keep core braced and avoid leaning back. Focus on shoulder blade retraction.
Safe exercise with no nerve stress. Maintain stable stance throughout.

name: Reverse Pec Deck (Rear-Delt Fly)
description: A machine-based rear-delt isolation exercise that targets the posterior deltoids while providing stable, supported positioning. Excellent for shoulder balance and preventing rounded shoulders.
instructions: Sit facing the pec deck machine with chest against pad. Grip handles with palms facing each other. Pull handles back by squeezing shoulder blades together. Keep slight bend in elbows. Control the return. Avoid using momentum.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Excellent choice with chest support. Keep torso stable against pad throughout.
Safe seated exercise with support. No nerve stress.

name: One-Arm Cable Bicep Curl (Facing Away)
description: A unilateral cable curl performed facing away from the stack. The arm starts slightly behind the body, providing a deep stretch on the biceps which is excellent for muscle growth while allowing the spine to remain upright and supported.
instructions: Set a single handle on a low pulley. Stand facing away from the cable in a split stance with the working arm slightly behind your body. Brace your core and keep your torso tall. Curl the handle toward your shoulder without swinging or leaning back, focusing on the deep stretch at the bottom. Control the descent and keep your elbow close to your side throughout.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Use a split stance and keep your torso upright. Avoid leaning back or using momentum to lift the weight. Choose a moderate load that allows strict control and pain-free motion.

name: One-Arm Cable Tricep Pushdown
description: A unilateral cable tricep pushdown that allows the user to hold the machine with the non-working hand for extra spinal stability. Ideal for isolating the triceps while keeping the lower back supported.
instructions: Attach a single handle to a high pulley. Stand close to the machine with one hand lightly holding the frame for support. With the working arm at your side and elbow bent to 90 degrees, push the handle down by straightening the elbow until the arm is almost fully extended. Keep your torso upright, core braced, and upper arm pinned to your side. Control the return and avoid shrugging the shoulder.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Use the free hand to hold the machine for extra spinal stability. Stand tall, avoid leaning over the weight stack, and use light to moderate load.

name: Cable Lateral Raise (Behind the Back)
description: A cable lateral raise variation where the cable runs behind the body, creating constant tension through the entire range of motion. Excellent for shoulder growth with a smooth resistance curve and minimal spinal loading.
instructions: Set a single handle on a low pulley. Stand sideways to the stack with the working arm furthest from the cable so the cable passes behind your hips. With a slight bend in your elbow, raise the handle out to the side until your hand reaches shoulder height. Pause, then lower slowly under control. Keep your torso upright and avoid leaning or swinging.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Use light weights and stand tall. Do not sway or hinge forward. Focus on shoulder movement only and stop if any back discomfort appears.

name: Bodyweight Box Squat
description: A controlled squat variation where you sit back to a box or bench. The fixed depth prevents going too low and avoids the "butt wink" (pelvic tuck) that can irritate the L5-S1 segment, making it very back-friendly.
instructions: Stand in front of a box or bench set to a height that allows you to sit with hips just above parallel. Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and brace your core. Sit back under control until you lightly touch the box, keeping your shins relatively vertical and spine neutral. Pause briefly, then drive through your heels to stand back up without rocking or collapsing onto the box.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Choose a box height that prevents the pelvis from tucking under at the bottom. Keep your spine neutral and avoid bouncing on the box. Excellent choice for teaching safe squat depth with disc issues.

name: Goblet Squat (Heels Elevated)
description: A goblet squat performed with the heels elevated on small plates or a wedge. Holding the dumbbell at the chest shifts the center of gravity forward and the heel elevation allows for a more vertical spine, making it very friendly for sensitive lower backs.
instructions: Place your heels on small plates or a wedge and stand shoulder-width apart. Hold a dumbbell vertically at your chest in a goblet position. Brace your core and sit straight down between your heels, keeping your torso as upright as possible. Lower to a comfortable depth without losing spinal neutrality, then drive through mid-foot and heel to stand back up.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Start with a light dumbbell and moderate heel elevation. The forward center of gravity helps maintain a vertical spine, reducing lumbar shear. Stop before any pelvic tuck or back discomfort.

name: Landmine Goblet Squat
description: A squat performed holding the end of a landmine bar in a goblet position. The fixed arc of the bar naturally guides you into an upright squat pattern, making it one of the safest squat options for disc issues and lower back sensitivity.
instructions: Secure one end of a barbell in a landmine attachment or corner. Stand close and hold the free end at chest level with both hands like a goblet. Brace your core and sit straight down between your feet as the bar moves in its fixed arc. Keep your torso tall and let the bar guide your path. Drive through your feet to stand back up without leaning forward.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: The fixed bar path makes it difficult to lean forward, reducing shear on the lumbar spine. Keep the bar close to the chest and avoid loading excessively heavy, especially in early rehab stages.

name: Single-Leg Box Step-Up
description: A unilateral lower-body exercise where you step onto a box or bench with one leg at a time. One of the best glute builders with almost zero spinal compression, making it ideal for people with lower back or disc issues.
instructions: Stand facing a box or bench roughly knee height. Place one foot fully on the box, brace your core, and drive through the front heel to stand tall on the box without pushing off the back leg. Control the descent back down on the same leg. Keep your torso upright and knee tracking over the middle of the foot. Perform all reps on one side before switching.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Keep torso upright and avoid using momentum from the trailing leg. Start with a lower box height and add load only when pain-free. Excellent choice for glute strength with minimal spinal compression.

name: Dead Bug
description: A core-stability exercise that trains anti-extension control by moving opposite arm and leg while maintaining a neutral lumbar spine. Excellent for back-safe core strength and motor control.
instructions: Lie on your back with hips and knees at 90 degrees and arms pointing toward the ceiling. Brace your core and keep your lower back gently pressed to the floor. Slowly extend one leg and the opposite arm without letting your back arch. Return to start and alternate sides. Move slowly with full control.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Keep lower back in contact with the floor at all times. Use short range if needed. Stop if any pain, pinching, or nerve symptoms appear.

name: Side Plank
description: An anti-lateral-flexion core exercise that strengthens the obliques, deep trunk stabilizers, and glute medius while placing minimal stress on the lumbar spine.
instructions: Lie on your side with elbow directly under shoulder and legs stacked or bottom knee bent for an easier version. Lift hips to create a straight line from shoulders to ankles or knees. Keep ribs down and pelvis neutral. Hold for time while breathing normally. Repeat on the other side.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Start with bent-knee side plank if needed. Keep hips high and avoid rotation. Stop immediately if symptoms radiate into the leg.

name: Cable Pallof Press
description: A cable anti-rotation exercise where you press the handle away from the body while resisting torso rotation. Excellent for trunk stability and reducing unwanted lumbar compensation.
instructions: Set cable at chest height and stand sideways to the machine. Hold the handle at your sternum with both hands and step away to create tension. Brace your core and press the handle straight out in front without letting your torso rotate. Pause briefly, then bring it back to chest under control. Perform both sides.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Use light to moderate resistance and prioritize no trunk rotation. Keep knees soft and pelvis neutral. Excellent choice for back-safe core training.

name: Farmer Carry
description: A loaded carry performed while walking with dumbbells or kettlebells in both hands. Builds total-body strength, grip, and core bracing with a highly functional and spine-friendly pattern when loads are controlled.
instructions: Pick up two weights and stand tall with shoulders down and back. Brace your core and walk with short controlled steps while maintaining upright posture. Keep ribs stacked over pelvis and avoid leaning to either side. Turn under control and set weights down safely.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Start very light and prioritize posture. No leaning, shrugging, or trunk sway. Stop if axial compression causes back pain and reduce load or distance.

name: Suitcase Carry
description: A unilateral loaded carry with weight in one hand only. Trains anti-lateral-flexion core strength and gait stability, making it highly useful for spinal control and back resilience.
instructions: Hold one dumbbell at your side and stand tall. Brace your core and walk forward without leaning toward or away from the load. Keep your shoulders level and hips stable. Walk for distance or time, then switch hands and repeat.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Begin with short distances and light load. Keep torso vertical and avoid side-bending. Reduce load if nerve symptoms or lumbar discomfort appear.

name: Standing Calf Raise
description: A lower-leg exercise targeting the gastrocnemius by raising the heels while standing. Improves ankle strength, lower-leg hypertrophy, and gait efficiency with minimal spinal loading.
instructions: Stand on a step or machine platform with the balls of your feet supported and heels free. Hold support lightly and brace your core. Rise onto your toes as high as possible, squeezing calves at the top. Lower slowly into a stretch without bouncing. Keep knees slightly soft and movement controlled.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Use support for balance and avoid loading the spine with heavy barbells. Keep torso upright and movement smooth.

name: Seated Calf Raise
description: A calf exercise performed seated with knees bent, emphasizing the soleus muscle. Useful for ankle strength, hypertrophy, and lower-leg endurance with very low spinal demand.
instructions: Sit on the machine with pads on your thighs and feet on the platform. Release safety handles, raise your heels as high as possible, and squeeze calves at the top. Lower slowly into a full stretch. Keep the movement smooth and avoid bouncing.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Seated support makes this very back-friendly. Keep posture neutral and avoid jerking the load.

name: Tibialis Raise
description: A lower-leg exercise targeting the tibialis anterior (front of shin). Helps improve ankle balance, deceleration control, and can reduce overuse stress on knees and calves.
instructions: Stand with your back against a wall and feet slightly in front, or use a tib bar. Keep heels on the floor and lift your toes toward your shins as high as possible. Pause briefly at the top, then lower under control. Keep movement slow and avoid rocking through the hips.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Very low spinal load. Maintain neutral posture against the wall and avoid compensating with trunk movement.

name: Chest-Supported Row
description: A machine-based horizontal pulling exercise where the chest rests against a pad, eliminating all spinal loading. The most back-friendly row variation — ideal for L5-S1 and disc issues because the thorax is fully supported and there is zero lumbar compression or shear.
instructions: Set the chest pad height so your chest rests flat against it with arms hanging freely. Grip the handles with palms facing each other or down. Pull the handles toward your torso by driving your elbows back, squeezing your shoulder blades together at the top. Lower with control to a full stretch. Keep chest against the pad throughout — do not lift off at the top.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Excellent choice — chest support removes all spinal loading. Keep chest flat on pad and avoid lifting the torso at the top. One of the safest back exercises for disc issues.
Highly recommended — no axial compression or lumbar shear. The chest pad fully supports the spine throughout the movement.
Safe option with no spinal loading. Stop if any nerve symptoms occur during the movement.

name: Seated Leg Curl
description: A hamstring isolation exercise performed on a seated leg curl machine. Training the hamstrings with the hip in a flexed position emphasizes the long head and produces more complete hamstring development compared to the lying variation. Zero spinal compression.
instructions: Sit on the machine with the back of your lower legs on the pad. Adjust so your knees align with the machine's axis. Hold the handles, brace your core, and curl your legs down as far as possible without lifting your hips. Squeeze at the bottom, then return slowly to the start. Avoid bouncing or swinging.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: No spinal loading — fully seated and supported. Keep hips down and avoid rocking the pelvis. Safe for virtually all L5-S1 conditions.

name: Seated Hip Adduction (Outer Side)
description: A machine-based inner-thigh isolation exercise that strengthens the hip adductors. Complements hip abduction work to build balanced pelvic stability, which supports proper spinal alignment and reduces compensatory lower back strain.
instructions: Sit tall in the machine with the pads against the inner sides of your knees and feet on the footrests. Keep your back against the support pad. Squeeze your legs together in a slow, controlled motion. Pause briefly at the fully closed position, then open back to the start under control. Avoid using momentum or arching your lower back.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Excellent seated exercise with full back support. Keep pelvis neutral and avoid twisting to either side. Safe for all L5-S1 conditions.

name: Standing Cable Hip Adduction (Inner Side)
description: A standing cable exercise that trains the hip adductors (inner thigh) through a full range of motion with constant cable tension. Strengthening the adductors improves pelvic stability and reduces compensatory lower back strain.
instructions: Attach an ankle strap to a low pulley and fasten it to the ankle of your working leg. Stand sideways to the machine with the working leg furthest from the stack. Hold the machine lightly for balance. Keeping your leg straight and torso upright, sweep the working leg across the body in front of the standing leg. Control the return to the start. Keep your hips level and core braced throughout.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Keep the torso vertical and avoid any side-bending or hip drop. Hold the machine for support. Use a light load and slow tempo to avoid pelvic shift.

name: Standing Cable Hip Abduction (Outer Side)
description: A standing cable exercise that trains the hip abductors (outer glute / glute medius) through a full range of motion with constant tension. Strengthening the glute medius improves single-leg stability, walking mechanics, and reduces lateral pelvic drop that stresses the lower back.
instructions: Attach an ankle strap to a low pulley and fasten it to the working leg. Stand sideways to the machine with the working leg closest to the stack. Hold the machine lightly for balance. Keeping your leg straight and torso upright, sweep the working leg away from the body out to the side. Pause at the top, then lower with control. Keep hips level and avoid leaning away from the machine.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Keep torso vertical and avoid trunk side-bend as compensation. Use the machine for support. Light resistance and controlled movement. Excellent for improving hip stability which protects the lumbar spine.

name: Seated Hip Adduction (Inner Side)
description: A machine-based inner-thigh isolation exercise that targets the hip adductors from a seated position with back support. Pulling the legs inward against resistance strengthens the adductor group, improving pelvic stability and reducing compensatory lower back strain during walking and standing.
instructions: Sit tall in the adductor machine with your back firmly against the pad and the leg pads positioned against the inner sides of your knees. Start with your legs open at a comfortable width. Squeeze your legs together in a slow, controlled motion, focusing on contracting the inner thigh muscles. Pause briefly at the fully closed position, then slowly open your legs back to the starting position under control. Keep your back flat against the pad and avoid using momentum or arching your lower back.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Excellent seated exercise with full back support. Keep pelvis neutral and avoid twisting to either side. The supported position makes this safe for all L5-S1 conditions.

name: Standing Cable Hip Adduction (Outer Side)
description: A standing cable exercise that trains the hip adductors through a cross-body pulling motion from the outer side. The constant cable tension throughout the full range of motion strengthens the inner thigh muscles, improving pelvic control, gait stability, and reducing compensatory lower back strain.
instructions: Attach an ankle strap to a low pulley and fasten it to the ankle of your working leg. Stand sideways to the machine with the working leg closest to the stack. Hold the machine lightly for balance. Keeping your leg straight and torso upright, sweep the working leg across the body past the standing leg. Control the return to the start position. Keep your hips level, core braced, and avoid leaning or rotating your torso throughout the movement.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Keep the torso vertical and avoid any side-bending or hip drop. Hold the machine for support. Use a light load and slow tempo to avoid pelvic shift. Safe for L5-S1 conditions with controlled form.

name: Standing Cable External Rotation
description: A shoulder stabilization exercise that targets the rotator cuff — specifically the infraspinatus and teres minor — to restore the shoulder's natural dynamic stability. The rotator cuff is responsible for centering the humeral head in the shoulder socket during all pressing and pulling movements. Weakness here leads to shoulder impingement, poor posture, and compensatory upper-back tension that often contributes to neck and lower back pain. This exercise directly retrains the stabilizers that protect the joint, making it essential for anyone with rounded shoulders, chronic upper-back tightness, or a history of shoulder or spine issues.
instructions: Set a cable pulley at elbow height. Stand sideways to the machine and grip the handle with the hand furthest from the stack. Tuck your elbow firmly against your side at 90 degrees and place a small rolled towel between your elbow and ribs to keep the upper arm locked in place. Slowly rotate your forearm away from your body as far as comfortable without shrugging or letting the elbow drift. Pause at end range, then return with full control. Only the forearm moves — the shoulder, torso and hips stay completely still. Use a very light load (2–4 kg). This is a stabilization drill, not a strength exercise.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Zero spinal loading. Keep the torso completely still — this is a shoulder stabilization drill, not a strength movement. Use 2–4 kg maximum. Strengthening the rotator cuff helps correct the rounded-shoulder posture that often drives chronic lower back compensation.

name: Front Plank
description: A foundational anti-extension core exercise that builds deep trunk stability by teaching you to resist lumbar arching under bodyweight load. Essential for spinal endurance, posture control, and reducing compensations that can aggravate low back pain.
instructions: Set up on forearms and toes with elbows under shoulders. Brace your core, squeeze glutes, and keep a straight line from head to heels. Maintain neutral ribs and pelvis, avoiding hip sag or piking. Hold steady while breathing slowly through the nose. Stop when form breaks.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Start with short holds (10-20 seconds) and prioritize perfect bracing. If pain appears, regress to knees-plank or dead bug.

name: Dumbbell Shrug
description: An upper-trapezius isolation exercise that improves shoulder girdle strength, posture support, and neck-shoulder resilience. Useful for balancing scapular musculature alongside rear-delt and mid-back work.
instructions: Stand tall holding dumbbells at your sides with arms straight. Keep chest up and core braced. Elevate shoulders straight up toward your ears without rolling them. Pause briefly at the top, then lower under control to full stretch. Avoid swinging, leaning, or neck jutting.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Use light to moderate loads and strict posture. Avoid heavy loading that increases axial compression or causes trunk sway.

name: Hammer Curl
description: A neutral-grip biceps variation that emphasizes the brachialis and brachioradialis, improving arm thickness, elbow resilience, and forearm strength. Complements standard supinated curls for more complete arm development.
instructions: Stand or sit tall with dumbbells at your sides, palms facing each other. Keep elbows close to your torso and curl both dumbbells upward without rotating the wrists. Squeeze at the top, then lower slowly to full extension. Keep shoulders down and avoid swinging the torso.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Perform seated or with back support if needed. Avoid momentum and keep core engaged.

name: Barbell Squat
description: A compound lower-body exercise performed with a barbell on the upper back. Targets the quads, glutes, and hamstrings while requiring significant core and spinal stabilization.
instructions: Position the barbell on your upper traps (high bar) or rear delts (low bar). Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and toes slightly out. Brace your core, take a deep breath, and descend by pushing your knees out and hinging at the hips simultaneously. Keep your chest up and spine neutral throughout. Descend until thighs are parallel to the floor or as depth allows. Drive through your heels to return to standing.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Avoid heavy loading with L5-S1 issues. If cleared by a specialist, use very light weight, maintain strict neutral spine, and avoid rounding the lower back at any point. Consider goblet squat or leg press as safer alternatives.

name: Barbell Bicep Curl
description: A classic isolation exercise for the biceps brachii performed standing with a barbell. Also engages the brachialis and brachioradialis with minimal spinal loading when performed with strict form.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart holding a barbell with an underhand (supinated) grip. Keep your elbows pinned close to your torso and your upper arms stationary. Curl the bar toward your shoulders by contracting the biceps. Pause briefly at the top, then lower the bar with full control. Avoid swinging the torso or using momentum.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Standing barbell curls create axial spinal compression — keep loads moderate (under 35% of bodyweight). Maintain a strict upright posture, do not lean back or swing the torso. Consider seated or preacher curl variations to reduce lumbar loading.

name: Barbell Shoulder Press
description: A compound overhead pressing exercise performed seated or standing with a barbell. Primarily targets the deltoids and triceps while demanding significant spinal stabilization under axial load.
instructions: Sit on an upright bench or stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Grip the barbell just outside shoulder width with an overhand grip. Unrack and hold at upper-chest level with elbows slightly in front of the bar. Brace your core and press the bar straight overhead until your arms are fully extended. Lower under control back to the starting position. Avoid arching the lower back — keep your ribcage down throughout.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Overhead pressing places significant compressive and shear forces on the lumbar spine, especially when standing. Avoid unless cleared by a specialist. If permitted, use the seated variation with back support, keep loads light, and maintain a strict neutral spine — no lumbar hyperextension at the top.

name: Lever Total Abdominal Crunch
description: A machine-based crunch performed on a lever abdominal machine. Targets the rectus abdominis through controlled spinal flexion against resistance, with seated back support and a fixed movement path.
instructions: Sit on the machine with your back against the pad and feet secured under the rollers. Grip the handles near your shoulders. Brace your core and crunch forward by contracting your abs, bringing your chest toward your hips. Pause briefly, then return slowly under control. Avoid pulling with the arms or jerking the weight.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Loaded spinal flexion can aggravate L5-S1 disc issues. Avoid unless cleared by a specialist. Consider dead bug or front plank as safer alternatives.
Repeated loaded flexion is contraindicated for herniated discs. Avoid this exercise.

name: Cable Kneeling Crunch
description: A kneeling cable crunch performed with a rope attachment on a high pulley. Targets the rectus abdominis through resisted spinal flexion, allowing progressive overload of the abs.
instructions: Attach a rope to a high pulley. Kneel facing the machine and hold the rope at the sides of your head. Hinge slightly at the hips, then crunch down by curling your torso toward your thighs using your abs. Keep hips stationary throughout. Pause at the bottom, then return slowly under control.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Loaded repeated flexion stresses lumbar discs. Avoid with L5-S1 issues. Use dead bug or Pallof press instead.
Contraindicated for herniated discs due to repeated loaded flexion.

name: Vertical Leg Raise (Captain's Chair, Bent Knee)
description: A core exercise performed on a captain's chair with forearms supported on pads. The bent-knee variation targets the lower abs and hip flexors while reducing lumbar strain compared to the straight-leg version.
instructions: Position yourself in the captain's chair with forearms on the pads and back against the backrest. Brace your core. Bend your knees and lift them toward your chest in a controlled motion. Avoid swinging. Lower with control and keep the lower back pressed against the pad.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Use bent-knee only. Keep pelvis neutral and lower back against the pad. Avoid swinging or going to full lumbar flexion.

name: Captains Chair Straight Leg Raise
description: An advanced core exercise performed on a captain's chair with straight legs. Strongly targets the lower abs and hip flexors but places significant demand on the lumbar spine.
instructions: Position yourself in the captain's chair with forearms on the pads and back firmly against the backrest. Brace your core hard. Keeping legs straight, raise them in front of you until parallel to the floor or higher. Avoid swinging. Lower slowly under control while keeping the lower back pressed against the pad.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Straight-leg raises create high shear forces on the lumbar spine via psoas pull. Avoid with L5-S1 issues. Use bent-knee variation instead.
Contraindicated for herniated discs. Heavy psoas activation pulls on lumbar vertebrae.

name: Lever Seated Crunch
description: A seated machine crunch that isolates the rectus abdominis through resisted spinal flexion. The seated, supported position provides a stable movement path with adjustable resistance.
instructions: Sit on the machine with your back against the pad and grip the handles over your shoulders. Brace your core and crunch forward by contracting your abs, curling your chest toward your hips. Pause at the bottom of the contraction, then return slowly under control. Avoid pulling with the arms.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Loaded spinal flexion stresses lumbar discs. Avoid with L5-S1 issues. Use anti-extension core work like dead bug or planks instead.
Contraindicated for herniated discs due to repeated loaded flexion.

name: Single-Arm Supported Dumbbell Curl
description: A unilateral biceps curl performed while the non-working arm braces against a bench, rack, or incline pad for torso support. By offloading the spine onto the supporting arm, this variation removes nearly all axial compression from the lumbar region, making it an excellent biceps builder for individuals with L5-S1 disc issues or sciatic nerve pain. The supported position also eliminates body sway and forces strict form, increasing biceps tension.
instructions: Stand next to a sturdy bench, incline pad, or squat rack. Place your non-working hand flat on the support and hinge slightly forward at the hips so your torso weight is transferred onto that supporting arm. Hold a dumbbell in the working hand with arm hanging straight down and palm facing forward. Brace your core lightly and curl the dumbbell toward your shoulder by contracting the biceps, keeping the upper arm stationary. Pause briefly at the top, then lower slowly under full control to a complete stretch. Keep the supporting arm locked throughout to maintain spinal offloading. Complete all reps on one side before switching.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Excellent choice for L5-S1 issues. The supporting arm transfers torso weight away from the lumbar spine, removing axial compression that occurs in standing curls. Keep the supporting arm firmly locked, avoid twisting the trunk, and use moderate weight with strict form.

Spine-friendly biceps option for sciatica sufferers. The supported hinge position offloads the lumbar spine and reduces nerve root compression compared to standing curls. Stop immediately if any radiating leg pain occurs.

Safer alternative to standing barbell or dumbbell curls. Torso support eliminates the axial loading that aggravates herniated discs. Maintain neutral spine throughout and avoid excessive forward rounding.

name: Single-Arm Supported Dumbbell Lateral Raise
description: A unilateral lateral raise performed while the non-working arm grips a sturdy upright (squat rack, pole, or machine frame) for support. Leaning slightly away from the support transfers torso weight onto the bracing arm, removing axial compression from the lumbar spine. This makes it a highly back-friendly side delt builder for individuals with L5-S1 disc issues or sciatic pain. The leaning angle also lengthens the side delt at the bottom, increasing range of motion and tension compared to standard standing raises.
instructions: Stand next to a sturdy upright such as a squat rack post, cable column, or machine frame. Grip the upright firmly with your non-working hand at about shoulder height. Lean your body away from the support so your weight is suspended on the bracing arm and your working side is fully extended. Hold a dumbbell in the working hand with arm hanging across the front of your body and a slight bend in the elbow. Raise the dumbbell out to the side in a controlled arc until your hand reaches shoulder height, leading with the elbow. Pause briefly at the top, then lower slowly under full control to a complete stretch. Keep the supporting arm locked throughout. Complete all reps on one side before switching.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Excellent choice for L5-S1 issues. The supporting arm transfers body weight away from the lumbar spine, removing the axial compression present in standing dumbbell lateral raises. Keep the supporting arm firmly locked, avoid trunk twisting, and use light to moderate loads with strict form.

Spine-friendly shoulder option for sciatica sufferers. The leaning supported position offloads the lumbar spine and reduces nerve root compression compared to standing raises. Stop immediately if any radiating leg pain occurs.

Safer alternative to standing lateral raises. Torso support eliminates the axial loading that aggravates herniated discs. Use light weight, maintain neutral spine, and avoid swinging or jerking the dumbbell up.

name: Romanian Deadlift (RDL)
description: A hip-hinge exercise that targets the hamstrings and glutes through a controlled eccentric stretch. Performed with a barbell or dumbbells, it is a cornerstone movement for posterior chain development.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, holding a barbell or dumbbells in front of your thighs. Hinge at the hips while keeping your back flat and knees slightly bent. Lower the weight along your legs until you feel a strong hamstring stretch, then drive your hips forward to return to standing. Keep the bar close to your body throughout.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Maintain strict neutral spine throughout. Avoid rounding the lower back at the bottom. Start with light loads and limit range of motion to a pain-free depth. Stop if any lumbar pain or radiating symptoms occur.
Stop if sciatic pain or leg tingling occurs. Keep range of motion within pain-free limits. Dumbbell variation with lighter loads is preferred over heavy barbell.
Perform only with clearance from a healthcare provider. Keep the spine neutral and avoid end-range lumbar flexion. Use moderate loads and prioritize hip hinge mechanics over depth.

name: Front Squat
description: A barbell squat variation with the bar racked on the front deltoids. The upright torso position reduces shear forces on the lumbar spine compared to the back squat, making it a more back-friendly quad-dominant compound lift.
instructions: Rack the barbell across your front deltoids with elbows high and parallel to the floor. Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes turned slightly out. Brace your core and descend by sitting the hips down and back while keeping the torso upright and elbows high. Drive through the floor to return to standing.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: The upright torso reduces lumbar load compared to back squat. Keep elbows high to maintain torso position. Avoid forward lean. Use moderate loads until form is established.
Preferable to back squat for sciatica sufferers due to more upright torso. Stop if radiating pain occurs. Keep depth within pain-free range.
Upright torso position reduces disc compressive forces versus back squat. Start with goblet squat pattern first, progress slowly. Stop if any back or leg symptoms appear.

name: Trap Bar Deadlift
description: A deadlift variation performed using a hexagonal (trap) bar. The lifter stands inside the bar and grips neutral handles at the sides, producing a more upright torso, reduced lumbar shear forces, and a safer loading pattern — making it the single most recommended deadlift variation for people with back issues.
instructions: Step inside the trap bar with feet hip-width apart. Push your hips back and bend your knees to grip the handles at your sides. Keep your chest up, back flat, and core braced. Drive through the floor, extending hips and knees simultaneously. Lock out at the top with hips fully extended. Lower the bar with control.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Highly recommended for L5-S1 issues. The neutral grip and upright torso significantly reduce lumbar shear compared to conventional deadlift. Start conservative with loads and ensure a neutral spine throughout.
Preferred deadlift variation for sciatica. The upright torso reduces nerve root stress. Stop immediately if leg tingling or sciatic pain occurs. Build weight gradually.
Best deadlift option for herniated disc. More upright torso reduces disc compressive load versus conventional or sumo deadlift. Prioritize form over load. Stop if any symptoms worsen.

name: Flat Barbell Bench Press
description: The foundational horizontal pressing movement. Performed lying flat on a bench, it is the most widely programmed chest exercise in strength training. Targets the pectorals, anterior deltoids, and triceps.
instructions: Lie flat on a bench with eyes under the bar. Grip the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width. Unrack with arms extended, lower the bar to mid-chest under control, then press back to full arm extension. Keep feet flat, shoulder blades retracted and depressed, and maintain a natural arch in the lower back.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Lying position offloads the lumbar spine. Safe for most L5-S1 conditions. Avoid excessive arching of the lower back. Keep feet flat on the floor or on a raised surface if needed.
Supine position is spine-neutral and generally safe for sciatica. Avoid excessive lower back arch during the lift. Use a spotter for heavy loads.
Lying flat removes axial spinal load. Generally well-tolerated with herniated disc. Avoid bridge-style excessive arching. Focus on shoulder blade retraction rather than lumbar hyperextension.

name: Bent-Over Barbell Row
description: A compound pulling movement performed with a barbell while hinging at the hips. One of the most effective back thickness builders, targeting the lats, rhomboids, traps, and rear deltoids.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart and grip the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width. Hinge forward until your torso is roughly 45–70 degrees to the floor, keeping your back flat. Pull the bar into your lower abdomen or upper hip by driving your elbows back. Lower with control. Keep the spine neutral throughout.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: High lumbar load in the bent-over position. Not recommended for active L5-S1 flare-ups. If cleared by a physician, use very light loads, a more upright torso angle, and keep reps low. Chest-supported row is a safer alternative.
Avoid during active sciatica flare-ups. The sustained hip-hinge position increases neural tension. Substitute with chest-supported or seated cable row.
Generally contraindicated with herniated disc due to high compressive and shear forces in the hip-hinge position under load. Use chest-supported row or single-arm dumbbell row instead.

name: Single-Arm Dumbbell Row
description: A unilateral horizontal pulling exercise performed with one hand and knee braced on a bench. Targets the lats, rhomboids, and traps while the bench support removes much of the lumbar load, making it one of the most back-friendly rowing variations.
instructions: Place your right knee and right hand on a flat bench for support. Hold a dumbbell in your left hand with arm hanging straight down. Pull the dumbbell toward your hip by driving your elbow back and up, keeping your torso parallel to the floor. Lower slowly to full arm extension. Complete all reps then switch sides.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Excellent back-friendly rowing option. Bench support significantly reduces lumbar load compared to bilateral bent-over rows. Keep the supported arm locked and torso parallel to the floor. Use moderate loads.
Bench support offloads the spine and reduces neural tension. Preferred horizontal pull for sciatica. Stop if any leg radiating pain occurs.
One of the safest rowing exercises for herniated disc. Braced position removes axial load from the spine. Avoid rotating the torso and keep core braced throughout.

name: Reverse Lunge
description: A unilateral lower-body exercise where you step backward into the lunge rather than forward. The reverse mechanics place less stress on the knee and reduce lumbar shear, making it more back- and knee-friendly than the forward lunge.
instructions: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart. Step one foot directly backward and lower your rear knee toward the floor, keeping your front shin vertical. Your front knee should stay above your ankle. Push through your front heel to return to standing. Alternate legs or complete all reps on one side. Can be loaded with dumbbells or a barbell.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Reverse lunge is more back-friendly than forward lunge. Keep torso upright and avoid forward lean. Start bodyweight only and progress to loaded versions cautiously.
Generally well-tolerated. Upright torso reduces nerve root stress. Stop if any radiating leg pain occurs during or after the movement.
Upright torso reduces disc compressive load. Preferred over forward lunge for herniated disc. Use bodyweight only initially and progress slowly.

name: Walking Lunge
description: A dynamic unilateral lower-body exercise where each step becomes a lunge, propelling you forward. Develops quads, glutes, and single-leg stability with a cardiovascular conditioning component.
instructions: Stand tall. Step forward with one foot and lower your rear knee toward the floor, keeping your front shin vertical. Drive through the front heel and step forward with the opposite foot into the next lunge. Continue alternating legs across the floor. Can be loaded with dumbbells at your sides.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Forward step creates more lumbar load than reverse lunge. Keep torso upright and take a conservative stride length. Avoid heavy loading. Prefer reverse lunge if pain is present.
The forward step and lateral stability demands can aggravate sciatica. Use bodyweight only. Stop if radiating pain occurs. Reverse lunge is a safer alternative.
Keep torso upright and stride length moderate. Use bodyweight only. If symptoms increase, switch to reverse lunge or stationary lunge.

name: Hack Squat
description: A machine-based squat variation where the back is supported against a padded sled at an angle. Delivers high quad stimulus with significantly reduced spinal loading, making it one of the most back-friendly heavy leg exercises available.
instructions: Load the machine and place your shoulders under the pads with your back flat against the sled. Feet are shoulder-width apart on the platform. Release the safety handles and descend by bending your knees until your thighs are parallel to the platform or below. Push through your heels to return to the start and re-engage the safeties.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Excellent back-friendly quad builder. Back support removes lumbar shear forces present in free-weight squats. Keep the back firmly pressed against the pad throughout. Avoid bouncing at the bottom.
Supported back position reduces sciatic nerve tension compared to free squats. Stop if leg tingling or radiating pain occurs. Avoid full depth if it aggravates symptoms.
Preferred over barbell squat for herniated disc. The back pad eliminates free axial loading of the spine. Start with light loads and conservative depth.

name: Nordic Hamstring Curl
description: A bodyweight eccentric hamstring exercise where you lower your torso toward the floor by resisting the pull of gravity on your hamstrings. Gold standard for hamstring eccentric strength and injury prevention.
instructions: Kneel on a mat with ankles secured under a pad, barbell, or held by a partner. Keeping your hips fully extended and body in a straight line from knees to head, lower your torso toward the floor as slowly as possible by resisting with your hamstrings. Use your hands to catch yourself at the bottom, then push back up and use hamstring strength to return as far as possible. Do not allow hips to flex during the movement.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Kneeling position with upright hips places minimal lumbar load. Strong hamstrings protect the lumbar spine. Keep hips fully extended throughout to avoid lumbar flexion compensation.
The extreme hamstring stretch at end-range may provoke sciatic tension. Start with partial range of motion and monitor symptoms. Stop if radiating pain occurs.

The extreme hamstring stretch at end range can provoke neural tension similarly to sciatica. Perform only with proven hip extension control. Start with partial range of motion and monitor for any radiating symptoms.

name: Machine Glute Kickback
description: A machine-based glute isolation exercise where one leg pushes a padded lever backward against resistance. Provides direct glute loading in a braced, supported position that keeps the lumbar spine unloaded.
instructions: Stand facing the machine and place your working leg's thigh pad behind your knee or lower thigh. Hold the support handles. Brace your core and push your leg backward and upward in a controlled arc, squeezing the glute at the top. Avoid hyperextending the lower back. Return slowly to the start. Complete all reps then switch legs.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Excellent glute isolator for L5-S1 issues. Machine support reduces lumbar loading. Avoid hyperextending the lower back at the top. Keep range of motion pain-free.
Supported glute activation safe for most sciatica presentations. Stop if radiating pain occurs. Focus on glute contraction rather than range of motion.
Safe for herniated disc. Machine support removes spinal load. Glute strengthening helps stabilize the lumbar spine long-term. Avoid excessive lumbar extension at peak contraction.

name: Flat Dumbbell Bench Press
description: A horizontal chest press performed with dumbbells on a flat bench. Allows a greater range of motion and more independent arm movement than the barbell version, with each side working unilaterally.
instructions: Lie flat on a bench holding a dumbbell in each hand at chest height, palms facing forward. Press both dumbbells upward to full arm extension above your chest, then lower slowly to the starting position with elbows at roughly 45–75 degrees from your torso. Maintain shoulder blade retraction throughout.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Supine position offloads the lumbar spine. Safe for L5-S1 conditions. Avoid excessive lower back arching. Use moderate loads and controlled tempo.
Lying flat is spine-neutral and generally safe for sciatica. Avoid aggressive back arch. Keep feet flat on the floor.
Supine position removes axial spinal load. Well-tolerated with herniated disc. Focus on shoulder blade retraction rather than lumbar hyperextension.

name: Arnold Press
description: A seated dumbbell shoulder press variation developed by Arnold Schwarzenegger. It begins with the palms facing you and rotates to a pronated position during the press, engaging all three deltoid heads through a fuller range of motion.
instructions: Sit on a bench with back support, holding dumbbells in front of your shoulders with palms facing your face and elbows forward. As you press upward, rotate your wrists outward so palms face forward at the top. Lower by reversing the rotation. Keep your lower back against the pad and avoid arching.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Seated with back support reduces lumbar load. Use a bench with full back support. Avoid arching the lower back during the press. Use moderate loads.
Seated supported position is spine-friendly. Back-supported bench preferred. Stop if any radiating symptoms occur.
Use a fully supported back rest. Avoid pressing to complete failure which can cause trunk compensation. Moderate loads with controlled tempo.

name: Preacher Curl
description: A biceps curl performed with the upper arms braced against a preacher bench pad. The arm support eliminates cheating and shoulder involvement, isolating the biceps in a fully supported, back-friendly position.
instructions: Sit at a preacher bench and drape your upper arms over the angled pad. Grip a barbell or EZ-curl bar with an underhand grip. Curl the weight up toward your shoulders by flexing the elbows, keeping the upper arms on the pad. Lower slowly to near-full extension. Avoid slamming the weight down at the bottom.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Seated and braced position eliminates spinal loading. Excellent choice for L5-S1 — upper arm support prevents any trunk swaying compensation.
Seated, arm-braced position is spine-neutral. Very safe for sciatica. No restrictions beyond general form cues.
One of the safest biceps exercises for herniated disc. Upper arm support on the pad eliminates trunk compensation entirely.

name: Concentration Curl
description: A seated, single-arm biceps curl where the elbow is braced against the inner thigh. Removes all momentum and trunk sway, providing strict biceps isolation in a back-friendly seated position.
instructions: Sit on a bench with legs spread. Hold a dumbbell in one hand and brace the back of that upper arm against your inner thigh. Curl the dumbbell up toward your shoulder with a full range of motion. Squeeze at the top, then lower slowly. Keep your torso stable and avoid leaning back.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Seated with arm braced against leg — no spinal loading. Excellent for L5-S1. Keep torso upright and avoid leaning forward excessively.
Sitting with elbow braced is spine-neutral. Very safe for sciatica. No specific restrictions.
Seated, braced position removes spinal loading entirely. Ideal for herniated disc patients who want upper body work without axial load.

name: Overhead Tricep Extension
description: A triceps isolation exercise performed with a dumbbell or cable with arms raised overhead. Targets the long head of the triceps through its fullest range of motion, which pushdowns and kickbacks cannot replicate.
instructions: Sit on a bench with back support and hold one dumbbell with both hands overhead, arms extended. Brace your core and keep your upper arms stationary next to your head. Lower the dumbbell behind your head by bending the elbows, then press back to full extension. Can also be performed with a cable and rope attachment.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Seated with back support is spine-friendly. Avoid arching the lower back when the arms are overhead. Engage the core lightly throughout.
Seated position is spine-neutral. Generally safe for sciatica. Use a fully supported back rest.
Seated, supported position is appropriate for herniated disc. Avoid heavy loads overhead that cause lumbar compensation. Keep ribs down.

name: Close-Grip Bench Press
description: A barbell bench press performed with a narrow grip (hands inside shoulder width). Shifts emphasis from the chest onto the triceps while still being a compound movement, performed in the same back-friendly supine position as the flat bench press.
instructions: Lie flat on a bench and grip the barbell with hands approximately shoulder-width apart or slightly narrower. Unrack with arms extended. Lower the bar to your lower chest / upper abdomen, keeping your elbows tucked at roughly 45 degrees. Press back to full extension. Avoid letting the elbows flare excessively.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Supine position offloads the lumbar spine identically to the flat bench press. Avoid excessive lower back arching. Safe for most L5-S1 conditions.
Lying flat is spine-neutral and well-tolerated for sciatica. Keep feet flat on the floor.
Supine position removes axial spinal load. Well-tolerated with herniated disc. Prioritize shoulder blade retraction over lumbar arch.

name: Upright Row
description: A vertical pulling movement performed with a barbell or dumbbells where the elbows rise above the hands to target the traps and lateral deltoids. A common trap and shoulder builder — note that shoulder internal rotation at the top may be contraindicated for those with impingement issues.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, holding a barbell with an overhand grip slightly narrower than shoulder-width. Pull the bar straight up along your body, leading with the elbows until the bar reaches upper chest or chin level. Lower with control. Keep the torso upright and avoid swinging.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Standing with load may compress the lumbar spine. Keep loads light to moderate. Avoid leaning back to complete reps. Use a slight forward torso lean to keep the bar close.
Standing loaded movement adds mild lumbar compressive load. Use light loads. Stop if radiating symptoms worsen. Seated cable upright row is a safer alternative.
Axial loading in the standing position may stress a herniated disc. Use light loads and strict form. Prefer seated or machine-based trap exercises.

name: T-Bar Row
description: A compound rowing movement performed on a T-bar machine or with a barbell in a landmine setup. A popular back thickness builder that allows heavier loading than dumbbell rows with a moderate lumbar demand.
instructions: Stand on the T-bar platform with feet shoulder-width apart and straddle the bar. Hinge forward to roughly 45 degrees, keeping your back flat. Grip the handles and pull the weight into your chest by driving your elbows back. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top. Lower with control. Keep the core braced and spine neutral throughout.

back_issue_restrictions.recommendation: Sustained hip-hinge position under load places high lumbar demand. Not recommended during active L5-S1 flare-ups. If cleared, use lighter loads and a more upright torso. Chest-supported T-bar is a safer variant.
Hip-hinge position increases neural tension. Avoid during acute sciatica. Substitute with chest-supported row or single-arm dumbbell row.
Contraindicated for active herniated disc. The hip-hinge under load generates high compressive and shear forces on the lumbar spine. Use chest-supported or machine rows instead.

name: Inverted Row
